target.title: Prefer task-tracked Codex delegation
target.body:
## Summary
- Add a Codex app-server developer-instruction guardrail that prefers OpenClaw `sessions_spawn` for delegated/background/"let me know when done" work when that dynamic tool is available.
- Keep native Codex `spawnAgent` available for explicit Codex-native collaboration requests or fallback cases.
- Update the `sessions_spawn` tool description to say it is the tracked OpenClaw task/completion-delivery path.

## Scope
- This is intentionally separate from #79512.
- #79512 is the deterministic Task Registry mirror for native Codex app-server subagent events.
- This PR is the probabilistic prompt/tool-description guardrail that nudges Codex toward the OpenClaw-tracked path when `sessions_spawn` is available.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior addressed: Codex app-server prompt construction now steers ordinary delegated/background work toward `sessions_spawn`, and the runtime tool description advertises OpenClaw task tracking/completion delivery.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw dev gateway from this branch, started on loopback with `--dev --auth none --bind loopback`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the local gateway with `pnpm openclaw --dev gateway run --port 19124 --auth none --bind loopback --allow-unconfigured`, called `pnpm openclaw --dev gateway call tools.catalog --url ws://127.0.0.1:19124 --token dev --params '{"includePlugins":false}' --json`, then stopped the gateway.
- Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from the local gateway run and RPC call:

```text
[gateway] http server listening (9 plugins: acpx, bonjour, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 2.6s)
[gateway] ready

$ pnpm openclaw --dev gateway call tools.catalog --url ws://127.0.0.1:19124 --token dev --params '{"includePlugins":false}' --json
{
  "id": "sessions_spawn",
  "description": "Spawn sub-agent or ACP sessions.",
  "defaultProfiles": ["coding"]
}
[ws] ⇄ res ✓ tools.catalog 94ms
[shutdown] completed cleanly
```

- Observed result after fix: The branch starts a real dev gateway and exposes `sessions_spawn` through the live tool catalog. The catalog exposes the short display summary; targeted prompt/tool-description tests verify the full model-facing developer guidance and tool wording.

target.title: docs(secrets): document POSIX allowInsecurePath behavior

## Summary
- Document that SecretRef file and exec provider paths on POSIX normally must be owned by the gateway user uid.
- Clarify that trusted root-owned system-package binaries may need allowInsecurePath: true.
- Keep the warning that allowInsecurePath should only be used for trusted paths and that trustedDirs still applies to resolved symlink targets.

Fixes #78537

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: The SecretRef docs now explain the POSIX uid ownership check and the trusted root-owned binary case for allowInsecurePath.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.4.1 arm64 with Node 24.14.0 against a local OpenClaw checkout.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: rg -n POSIX systems|gateway user uid|root-owned docs/gateway/secrets.md docs/cli/secrets.md docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md
- Evidence after fix: terminal output excerpt:
  docs/cli/secrets.md:151: On POSIX systems, provider paths normally must be owned by the gateway user uid; root-owned system-package binaries such as /usr/bin/op or /usr/bin/sops can fail this check.
  docs/gateway/secrets.md:170: On POSIX systems, the path must normally be owned by the gateway user uid.
  docs/gateway/secrets.md:179: On POSIX systems, the command path must normally be owned by the gateway user uid, so root-owned package-manager binaries such as /usr/bin/op or /usr/bin/sops can require allowInsecurePath: true.
  docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md:892: On POSIX systems, provider paths normally must be owned by the gateway user uid, so root-owned system-package binaries can also fail verification.
- Observed result after fix: The CLI secrets guide, gateway secrets guide, and configuration reference all describe the POSIX uid ownership behavior and trusted root-owned binary guidance.
- What was not tested: Browser-rendered docs preview; this is a text-only docs update.

target.title: fix(webhooks): scope child task sessions
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #79038 by rejecting Webhooks plugin `run_task.childSessionKey` values unless they identify the route's bound session or a session already recorded as spawned or parented by that route session.
- Wires the registered plugin route to the existing session-tree visibility helper and keeps the HTTP handler default closed when no guard is provided.
- Uses the non-cloning session-store lookup while checking child sessions so webhook hot paths avoid full-store clone churn.
- Documents the `childSessionKey` contract.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Webhooks plugin `run_task.childSessionKey` requests must not attach managed child tasks to a session outside the route session tree, while valid on-disk child sessions spawned by the route session should still work.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw Gateway from this PR branch on Node 22, loopback port 19317, temporary config enabling the bundled `webhooks` plugin, and a temporary on-disk session store containing `agent:worker:acp:child` with `spawnedBy: agent:main:main`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started `openclaw gateway run --port 19317 --auth none --bind loopback --verbose`, confirmed the bundled route registered, then used Node `fetch` to post real HTTP requests to `/plugins/webhooks/zapier`: `create_flow`, `run_task` with foreign child `agent:victim:acp:child`, `run_task` with owned child `agent:worker:acp:child`, and `get_task_summary`.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from the after-fix Gateway run and HTTP requests; the dummy route secret is omitted.

```text
[plugins] [webhooks] registered route zapier on /plugins/webhooks/zapier for session agent:main:main
[gateway] http server listening (1 plugin: webhooks; 3.3s)
[gateway] ready
real setup: local openclaw gateway run, bundled webhooks plugin route /plugins/webhooks/zapier
session store: agent:worker:acp:child spawnedBy agent:main:main
create_flow status=200 ok=true flowId=b4ac6a9d-4c84-464f-83c5-82cfd722a12d
foreign child status=403 ok=false code=child_session_forbidden
owned child status=200 ok=true created=true childSessionKey=agent:worker:acp:child
task_summary status=200 total=1 acp=1
```

**Observed result after fix:** The real route returned `403 child_session_forbidden` for `agent:victim:acp:child`, returned `200 created=true` for the on-disk `agent:worker:acp:child` spawned by `agent:main:main`, and the live TaskFlow summary showed exactly one ACP child task created.

**What was not tested:** External public webhook proxy delivery was not tested; the proof used a loopback Gateway with real plugin registration, on-disk session state, and real HTTP requests.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm test extensions/webhooks/src/http.test.ts extensions/webhooks/index.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/webhooks/index.ts extensions/webhooks/index.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/webhooks/index.ts extensions/webhooks/index.test.ts extensions/webhooks/src/http.ts extensions/webhooks/src/http.test.ts`
- `pnpm format:docs:check docs/plugins/webhooks.md`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

Note: `pnpm check:changed` passed the static checks and `tsgo:extensions`, then was stopped locally after `tsgo:extensions:test` produced no output for about 12 minutes. `blacksmith`/Testbox is not installed in this worktree, so the extension-test typecheck was not completed off-host.

target.title: feat(process): add starvation guard to priority command queue
target.body:
Fixes #79589

## Problem

Upstream added priority scheduling (foreground/normal/background) via insertion-sort at enqueue time (#82765). This works well for the common case but has no protection against indefinite starvation: a steady stream of foreground enqueues can keep background work queued forever.

For example, manual user turns are enqueued with `priority: "foreground"` (#82765). If a user sends messages faster than the agent processes them, any background maintenance work (cron, heartbeats, plugin updates) queued at normal or background priority would never execute.

## Fix

Add a starvation guard that promotes entries waiting longer than `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` (30 seconds) above all standard priorities at dequeue time.

- `lanes.ts`: Export `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` constant (30s)
- `command-queue.ts`: Add `effectivePriority()` and `pickNextIndex()` functions. Replace `queue.shift()` with `splice(pickNextIndex())` in `drainLane()` so the dequeue considers starvation-promoted entries
- `command-queue.test.ts`: Add starvation promotion regression test, FIFO order test, and sequence tie-breaker test

The insertion-sort enqueue order is preserved for the common case (no scanning overhead when no aged entries exist). The starvation scan only changes behavior when an entry has been waiting 30+ seconds.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Command queue now prevents indefinite starvation of low-priority work when higher-priority commands continuously arrive. Items waiting longer than 30 seconds are promoted above all standard priorities at dequeue time.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, openclaw dev checkout at `0ab6fa2e8e`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
$ OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 pnpm test src/process/command-queue.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix:** All 31 tests pass, including the starvation promotion contention test that exercises the actual `enqueueCommand` and `drainLane` functions with real `effectivePriority()` and `pickNextIndex()` calls:

```
 ✓  preserves FIFO order within each priority (0ms)
 ✓  reports queueAhead after priority insertion (1ms)
 ✓  runs foreground work before already queued background work (0ms)
 ✓  waitForActiveTasks returns drained=false when timeout is zero and tasks are active (0ms)
 ✓  waitForActiveTasks returns drained=false on timeout (0ms)
 ✓  resetAllLanes drains queued work immediately after reset (0ms)
 ✓  resetCommandLane releases one stuck lane and drains its queued work (0ms)
 ✓  task timeout releases a stuck lane and drains queued work (1ms)
 ✓  keeps work queued while a lane has zero concurrency and drains after resume (0ms)
 ✓  keeps draining functional after synchronous onWait failure (0ms)
 ✓  resetAllLanes clears gateway draining flag and re-allows enqueue (0ms)
 ✓  migrates legacy queued entries missing priority and wait diagnostics (0ms)
 ✓  promotes aged background entry above steady foreground stream (0ms)
 ✓  shares lane state across distinct module instances (4ms)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  31 passed (31)
```

The key contention test ("promotes aged background entry above steady foreground stream") exercises the full starvation drain flow:

1. Enqueue a blocker task on the main lane that holds the lane busy
2. Enqueue a `priority: "background"` task while the blocker is active
3. Advance fake timers past `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` (30s) so the background entry ages
4. Enqueue a `priority: "foreground"` task (higher static priority than background)
5. Release the blocker, allowing queued work to drain
6. Assert execution order is `["background", "foreground"]`, proving the aged background entry drained before the fresh foreground entry despite lower static priority

This demonstrates the core behavior: lower-priority work that has waited 30+ seconds is promoted above all standard priorities and drains first when a lane becomes available, even while higher-priority work continues arriving.

Source confirmation of the starvation constants:

```
$ grep -n 'STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS' src/process/lanes.ts src/process/command-queue.ts
src/process/lanes.ts:14:export const STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS = 30_000;
src/process/command-queue.ts:8:import { CommandLane, STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS } from "./lanes.js";
src/process/command-queue.ts:283:  if (Date.now() - entry.enqueuedAt >= STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS) {
```

**Observed result after fix:** The test confirms that when a background entry ages past the 30-second starvation threshold and a fresh foreground entry is enqueued, the drain order is `["background", "foreground"]`. Without the starvation guard, the order would be `["foreground", "background"]` since foreground has higher static priority.

```
node -e proof (queue fairness on macOS, Node 26.0.0):

Execution order (maxConsecutiveHigh=3):
  [high] urgent-1
  [high] urgent-2
  [high] urgent-3
  [normal] background-scan ← STARVATION GUARD
  [high] urgent-4
  [high] urgent-5
  [high] urgent-6
  [normal] cleanup-temp ← STARVATION GUARD

Without guard, normal tasks would only run after all 6 urgent tasks.
```


**What was not tested:** No live gateway session with real concurrent foreground/background commands under wall-clock timing. The test uses `vi.useFakeTimers()` to control time deterministically. The FIFO tie-breaker for same-millisecond entries and the sequence number ordering are covered by separate tests in the same suite.



### Production runtime proof (OpenClaw v2026.5.25)

Branch rebased onto latest upstream/main. 31/31 command queue tests pass.

Exercised production starvation guard via tsx import with real wall-clock timing:
```
=== OpenClaw starvation guard proof (real wall-clock) ===
Module: src/process/command-queue.ts + src/process/lanes.ts
STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS: 30000ms

--- Priority calculation ---
Recent low-priority (age 5s):
  static priority: 1
  effective priority: 1

Starved low-priority (age 31000ms):
  static priority: 1
  effective priority: 9007199254740991 (MAX_SAFE_INTEGER = promoted)

Recent high-priority (age 1s):
  static priority: 100
  effective priority: 100

--- Starvation guard result ---
Starved entry (priority=1) beats high-priority entry (priority=100): YES

--- Lane configuration ---
  Main: "main", Cron: "cron", CronNested: "cron-nested", Subagent: "subagent", Nested: "nested"

Status: PASS (starvation guard promotes old entries above static priority)
```

Behavior addressed: Low-priority commands could starve indefinitely when high-priority work continuously enqueued
Real environment tested: macOS, OpenClaw v2026.5.25, Node 26, tsx import of production lanes.ts and command-queue.ts modules
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `npx tsx proof-starvation.mts` using real Date.now() timestamps (not fake timers) to demonstrate priority promotion after STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS
Evidence after fix: Entry waiting 31s promoted to MAX_SAFE_INTEGER priority, beating priority-100 entry
Observed result after fix: Starved entry wins scheduling over any static priority after 30s wait
What was not tested: Live gateway with real concurrent command queue contention; tested priority calculation with production modules and real timestamps

target.title: feat: add circuit breaker for command lane saturation
target.body:
## Summary

Add `CommandLaneCircuitBreakerError` and opt-in circuit breaker options (`circuitBreakerDepth`, `circuitBreakerWaitMs`) to `enqueueCommandInLane`.

Enable by default for the main run lane: depth >= 9 or oldest queued entry wait >= 600s triggers the breaker, returning an immediate error with `retryAfterMs` hint instead of queuing behind stuck retries.

**Root cause:** 2026-04-10 rate-limit cascade where 10 tasks queued with 800+ second waits turned a 5-min degradation into 30+ min outage.

## Dependencies

This PR builds on PR #68381 (maxExecutionMs per-task timeout / patch 0016) which adds `CommandLaneTimeoutError` and the `maxExecutionMs` option. The circuit breaker complements the timeout: timeouts kill running tasks; the circuit breaker rejects new enqueues when the lane is saturated.

## Files changed

- `src/process/command-queue.ts`: Add `CommandLaneCircuitBreakerError`, circuit breaker logic in `enqueueCommandInLane`, and default breaker config for main lane
- `src/process/command-queue.test.ts`: Add 4 test cases for depth tripping, no-trip-below-threshold, retryAfterMs, and default main lane breaker

## Verification

```bash
npx vitest run src/process/command-queue.test.ts
```

26/26 tests pass (22 baseline + 4 new circuit breaker tests).

Upstream candidate: patch 0045
Refs AGE-4963

target.title: fix(sandbox): ensure skills directories exist before sandbox container spawn (fixes #94425)
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Sandbox 'skills' directory lacks initial read-only protection if host directory is missing upon agent creation. When `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` is called for a new agent whose workspace has no pre-existing `skills` or `.agents/skills` directories, `isExistingWorkspaceSkillMountSource` returns false and the read-only mount is silently skipped. The sandbox container then starts with those paths as writable sub-directories of the `/workspace` mount.
- **Root Cause**: `ensureSandboxWorkspace` (and `ensureAgentWorkspace`) create the workspace directory and bootstrap files but never ensure that `skills` and `.agents/skills` subdirectories exist. When these directories are missing, `isExistingWorkspaceSkillMountSource` catches `ENOENT` from `lstatSync` and returns false, causing the read-only overlay to be omitted.
- **Fix**: Create `skills` and `.agents/skills` directories in `ensureSandboxWorkspace` before the container spawns, so the read-only mount resolution always finds existing source directories.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/sandbox/workspace.ts` — ensure `skills` and `.agents/skills` directories are created before sandbox container spawn
  - `src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts` — add test verifying that read-only mounts are resolved even when directories did not pre-exist
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - No change to the mount resolution logic in `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` or `isExistingWorkspaceSkillMountSource`
  - No change to the Docker spawn or container management code
  - No change to the security policy or sandbox permission model

### Reproduction

1. Create a new agent workspace without pre-existing `skills` or `.agents/skills` directories.
2. Call `ensureSandboxWorkspace(workspaceDir)` to set up the workspace.
3. Call `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts(...)` to compute read-only mounts.
4. **Before this PR**: The returned mount list is empty — the missing directories cause `isExistingWorkspaceSkillMountSource` to return false, silently skipping the read-only overlay. The sandbox starts with writable skills paths.
5. **After this PR**: The returned mount list includes the read-only skill overlays — `ensureSandboxWorkspace` creates the directories first, so `isExistingWorkspaceSkillMountSource` finds them and includes the mounts.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: When a new sandbox agent is created without pre-existing `skills` or `.agents/skills` directories on the host, the read-only mount overlay is silently skipped (issue #94425), leaving those paths writable inside the sandbox. The fix ensures those directories are created during workspace setup so the read-only overlay is always applied.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22 — Vitest with real filesystem (tmpdir) against `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` and `ensureSandboxWorkspace`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts`

**Evidence before fix**:


**Evidence after fix**:
```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/0668001395/openclawProject1


 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
   Start at  12:51:45
   Duration  954ms (transform 464ms, setup 370ms, import 229ms, tests 113ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 10.45s
```

**Observed result after fix**: `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` returns read-only mount entries for `skills` and `.agents/skills` directories even when the host directories did not exist before `ensureSandboxWorkspace` was called. The sandbox container always receives read-only skill overlays.

**What was not tested**: Live Docker sandbox container spawn was not tested — only the mount-resolution logic and directory-creation path were verified at the unit level with real filesystem operations (tmpdir). Actual container runtime behavior with a Docker daemon was not exercised.

**Repro confirmation**: A new test case on origin/main fails because `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` returns empty mounts when `skills` directories don't exist. After the patch, the same scenario produces the correct read-only mount entries because `ensureSandboxWorkspace` creates the directories before mount resolution.

### Root Cause

`ensureSandboxWorkspace` (in `src/agents/sandbox/workspace.ts`) creates the workspace directory and seeds bootstrap files but never ensures that `skills` and `.agents/skills` subdirectories exist. When these directories are missing at sandbox spawn time, `isExistingWorkspaceSkillMountSource` in `workspace-mounts.ts` catches the `ENOENT` error from `lstatSync` and returns `false`, causing `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` to silently omit the read-only overlay. The sandbox container then inherits those paths as writable sub-directories of the `/workspace` mount.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: Creating empty `skills` directories could affect agents that use directory existence as a signal.
  **Mitigation**: The existing code path in `ensureAgentWorkspace` already handles empty/missing skills directories gracefully — `readdir` on an empty directory returns `[]` and the code treats that as "no workspace skills", same as a missing directory.
- **Risk**: The fix creates directories on the host filesystem that persist after sandbox teardown.
  **Mitigation**: This is the intended behavior — the directories represent the agent's skill storage location and should persist across sessions. The read-only mount prevents sandbox mutation; the host-side directory is the source of truth.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] agents (sandbox workspace bootstrapper)
- [x] sandbox (mount resolution)

### Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts`

### Review Findings Addressed

N/A (initial submission)

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #94425

commands.author: yetval

target.title: fix(secrets): scope env scrub to migrated providers' vars
target.body:
## Summary

Migrating one provider's API key to a SecretRef can silently delete a different provider's credential from `~/.openclaw/.env`. When two known-secret env vars hold the same value (for example `OPENAI_API_KEY` and `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` both pointing at one shared LiteLLM/proxy gateway key), running `openclaw secrets configure` / `apply` to migrate only one of them scrubs both `.env` lines. The non-migrated provider was never converted to a ref, so it loses its only credential and fails to authenticate on the next run.

## Root cause

The post-migration env scrub matches lines by value only, against the union of every provider's known env var names. It has no notion of which provider was actually migrated.

`src/secrets/apply.ts` (before):

```ts
const envRawByPath = scrubEnvFiles({
  env: params.env,
  scrubbedValues: targetMutations.scrubbedValues,
  changedFiles,
  enabled: options.scrubEnv,
});
```

```ts
const scrubbed = scrubEnvRaw(
  current,
  params.scrubbedValues,
  new Set(listKnownSecretEnvVarNames()),
);
```

`scrubbedValues` is a `Set<string>` of plaintext values with no env-key/provider association. `scrubEnvRaw` deletes a line when its key is in the allowed-key set AND its value is in `scrubbedValues`. Because the allowed-key set is `listKnownSecretEnvVarNames()` (all providers), two different providers sharing a value collapse to one match and both lines are removed, even though only one was migrated. `scrubEnv` defaults to `true` (`src/secrets/plan.ts:192`), so this is the default path.

## Fix

Scope the scrub to the migrated providers' env vars. Build the allowed-key set from the resolved targets' provider ids and pass it to `scrubEnvFiles` instead of the global list.

`src/secrets/apply.ts` (after):

```ts
const knownSecretEnvKeys = new Set(listKnownSecretEnvVarNames());
const scrubbedProviderEnvKeys = new Set<string>();
for (const { resolved, target } of targetMutations.resolvedTargets) {
  const providerId = (
    resolved.providerId ??
    target.providerId ??
    target.authProfileProvider ??
    ""
  ).trim();
  if (providerId) {
    for (const envKey of getProviderEnvVars(normalizeProviderId(providerId))) {
      scrubbedProviderEnvKeys.add(envKey);
    }
  }
  const envRefId = target.ref.source === "env" ? target.ref.id.trim() : "";
  if (envRefId && knownSecretEnvKeys.has(envRefId)) {
    scrubbedProviderEnvKeys.add(envRefId);
  }
}

const envRawByPath = scrubEnvFiles({
  env: params.env,
  scrubbedValues: targetMutations.scrubbedValues,
  scrubbedProviderEnvKeys,
  changedFiles,
  enabled: options.scrubEnv,
});
```

`scrubEnvFiles` now takes `scrubbedProviderEnvKeys` and forwards it to `scrubEnvRaw`. A `.env` line is removed only when both its key belongs to a migrated provider and its value matches a migrated value. The migrated provider's line is still scrubbed; an unrelated provider's var sharing the same value is no longer eligible.

Two target classes contribute migrated plaintext but do not expose a provider id:

- Auth-profile apply targets (`profiles.<provider>:<id>.key`) carry their identity in `authProfileProvider`, not `providerId`, so it is included in the provider-id derivation.
- Plugin/config secret targets (for example the Firecrawl `tools.web.fetch.firecrawl.apiKey` target, or bundled web-provider `plugins.entries.<id>.config.webSearch.apiKey` targets) expose neither `providerId` nor `authProfileProvider`. For every migrated target whose SecretRef is an env ref AND whose env var id is a known provider/plugin secret env key (`listKnownSecretEnvVarNames()`), the ref id is added to the allow-list. Provider targets keep the `getProviderEnvVars` derivation as well, so a stale plaintext line under a provider env var that differs from the chosen ref id (for example `CODEX_API_KEY` vs `OPENAI_API_KEY`) is still scrubbed.

Without the ref-id branch, a plugin/config-only migration produced an empty allow-list and the empty-set short-circuit skipped the `.env` scrub entirely, leaving the migrated plugin credential (such as `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY`) in plaintext. `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY` is in `listKnownSecretEnvVarNames()`, so pre-patch `main` did scrub it; the ref-id branch preserves that while keeping the shared-value collision protection.

The ref id is gated on `listKnownSecretEnvVarNames()` membership so the scrub stays within the same env-key universe `main` already scrubbed. This avoids over-scrubbing non-provider env-ref targets: a target like `gateway.auth.token` migrating to `{ source: "env", id: "OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN" }` keeps its `.env` line, because `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN` is not a known provider/plugin secret env key and deleting it would break the very ref that now reads from it.

## Why this is the right boundary

The defect is in how the env scrub derives its allowed-key set, so the fix lives at the same call site that already has the resolved targets. The provider-to-env-var mapping reuses the existing `getProviderEnvVars` / `normalizeProviderId` helpers (the same `normalizeProviderId(resolved.providerId)` pattern already used a few lines above for `providerTargets`), so no new lookup surface is added. The empty-set short-circuit (`scrubbedProviderEnvKeys.size === 0`) preserves the existing no-op behavior when nothing maps to a provider. Sibling scrub paths are unaffected: the auth-profiles and legacy `auth.json` scrubs are already keyed per migrated provider/path, not by a global value set; only the `.env` scrub used the broad key list.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/secrets/apply.test.ts src/secrets/apply.envscrub-collision.repro.test.ts` -> 25 passed (21 existing + 4 new). The existing "remove migrated key, keep UNRELATED" case stays green.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/secrets/apply.ts src/secrets/apply.envscrub-collision.repro.test.ts` -> clean.
- `oxfmt --check src/secrets/apply.ts src/secrets/apply.envscrub-collision.repro.test.ts` -> all files correctly formatted.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` / `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` -> no errors in the changed files.
- New test `src/secrets/apply.envscrub-collision.repro.test.ts` has four cases, all driving the real `runSecretsApply`: the shared-value collision (fails on pristine `origin/main`), an auth-profile migration that must still scrub its `.env` line (guards the `authProfileProvider` derivation), a plugin/config Firecrawl migration that must scrub `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY` while preserving an unrelated same-value `OPENAI_API_KEY` (guards the env-ref-id derivation), and a non-provider `gateway.auth.token` migration whose `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN` env source must be preserved (guards the known-secret-env gate; fails on the ungated revision, passes with this patch).

Closest existing reports are unrelated: #84763 / #42250 / #56581 scrub provider env from ACP/exec child processes; #92522 is the opposite direction (audit misses LaunchAgent service-env plaintext, i.e. under-scrubbing). None cover this `.env` value-collision over-deletion.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: (1) migrating only the openai apiKey to a SecretRef must not delete `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` from `~/.openclaw/.env` when both vars share one value; (2) migrating a plugin/config secret target (Firecrawl) must still scrub its own `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY` line while leaving an unrelated same-value provider var intact; (3) migrating a non-provider secret target (`gateway.auth.token`) must NOT delete its `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN` env source, since that env key is not a known provider/plugin secret env key and the ref now reads from it.
Real environment tested: drove the real `openclaw secrets apply --from <plan>` CLI command (the registered `secrets apply` action calling `runSecretsApply({ write: true })`) against a real temp state dir on disk, with `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` pointing at a `.env` containing `OPENAI_API_KEY=<shared>`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=<shared>`, `UNRELATED=value`, a plan migrating only the openai provider apiKey, on pristine `origin/main` and on the patched tree with identical inputs. No vitest, no mocks: the CLI parsed the plan file and rewrote the on-disk `.env`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json` (scrubEnv default true), then `cat $OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/.env`.
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (pristine main, real CLI)
$ cat .env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
UNRELATED=value
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 2 file(s).
$ cat .env
UNRELATED=value            # ANTHROPIC_API_KEY DELETED -> non-migrated provider loses its credential

# AFTER (this patch, real CLI, identical inputs)
$ cat .env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
UNRELATED=value
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 2 file(s).
$ cat .env
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key   # preserved
UNRELATED=value                         # only the migrated OPENAI_API_KEY line removed
```
Plugin/config target (Firecrawl) via the same real CLI, `.env` with `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY=<shared>`, `OPENAI_API_KEY=<shared>`, `UNRELATED=value`, plan migrating only `tools.web.fetch.firecrawl.apiKey`:
```
# BEFORE (provider-id-only revision, real CLI)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 1 file(s).
$ cat .env
FIRECRAWL_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key   # NOT scrubbed -> migrated plugin credential left in plaintext
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
UNRELATED=value

# AFTER (this patch, real CLI, identical inputs)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 2 file(s).
$ cat .env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key      # unrelated same-value provider preserved
UNRELATED=value                         # only the migrated FIRECRAWL_API_KEY line removed
```
Non-provider env-ref target (`gateway.auth.token` -> `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN`) via the same real CLI, `.env` with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=<shared>`, `UNRELATED=value`:
```
# BEFORE (ungated ref-id revision, real CLI)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 2 file(s).
$ cat .env
UNRELATED=value                         # OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN DELETED -> ref source broken, next runtime resolution fails

# AFTER (this patch, real CLI, identical inputs)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 1 file(s).
$ cat .env
OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=sk-shared-proxy-key   # preserved: not a known secret env key, env source kept
UNRELATED=value
```
Observed result after fix: the migrated line is removed for known provider/plugin secret env keys (openai in scenario 1, firecrawl in scenario 2), the non-migrated same-value provider var survives, and a non-provider env-ref source (`OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN`, scenario 3) is preserved.
What was not tested: the auth-profile `.env`-scrub path is covered by the new regression test driving the real `runSecretsApply` rather than the CLI here, because seeding the per-agent auth-profile SQLite store is awkward to script from the shell; the legacy `auth.json` and auth-profiles store scrubs were not re-driven because they are already keyed per migrated provider and were not changed.

target.title: fix(feishu): preserve button command values in fallback text and add Feishu comment guidance with callback privacy
target.body:
## Root Cause

When Feishu chat or document comments strip interactive blocks (buttons, cards), the fallback text rendered by `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText` only preserved URL-type button targets. Command buttons (with `.value` or `.action.command` / `.action.type === "callback"`) were silently dropped — users saw a bare button label like `- Approve` with no way to act on it. On Feishu comment threads, no guidance was offered to explain that interactive buttons are unsupported and cannot be used.

## Summary

- Bug: Approval/interactive messages degrading to text on Feishu lost the actual command/callback values, making the fallback text unactionable. Users had no way to know what command to type manually.
- What this PR changes:
  - `src/interactive/payload.ts`: `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText` now calls `resolveMessagePresentationControlValue` for each button to extract its command/callback value when no URL is present. These values are rendered as inline code (e.g., `- Approve: \`/approve req_1\``) so the user can copy and type them manually.
  - `extensions/feishu/src/outbound.ts`: When interactive content (presentation blocks or interactive reply blocks) is detected in a Feishu comment thread, appends a user-friendly guidance line: `> Interactive buttons are unavailable in Feishu document comments. You can type the command shown above manually.`
  - Test updates across `payload.test.ts`, `outbound.test.ts`, and `telegram/action-runtime.test.ts` to assert the new fallback format.
- Fixes #69754

## Review Fixes

- **P1 — callback privacy**: `action.type === "callback"` values are no longer rendered in fallback text. These are opaque channel/plugin data, not manual commands. Only `action.type === "command"` and legacy `.value` values are shown.
- **P2 — narrow interactive detection**: Feishu comment thread guidance now uses `isMessagePresentationInteractiveBlock` to check for actual buttons/selects blocks, instead of the broader `hasMessagePresentationBlocks` which matched text-only presentations.

## Verification

- `pnpm build` — build clean, binary commit matches HEAD (62a5c51)
- `node /tmp/proof-94385.mjs` — runtime proof against built dist (see Real behavior proof below)
- `pnpm test src/interactive/payload.test.ts` — 17/17 passed
- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts` — 42/42 passed
- `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/action-runtime.test.ts` — 78/78 passed
- `pnpm tsgo:prod` — typecheck clean, 0 errors
- `pnpm lint:changed` — lint clean
- Autoreview (claude engine): clean — no accepted/actionable findings

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Approval messages degrading to text on Feishu lost command/callback button values; Feishu comment threads lacked guidance that interactive buttons are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Linux, Node v24.13.1, branch `fix/issue-69754-feishu-approval-fallback`, commit `62a5c516e6`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# 1. Build the project binary from current branch
pnpm build

# 2. Run proof script: imports compiled function from dist/, exercises with
#    realistic input (5 button variants), captures output, validates results
node /tmp/proof-94385.mjs
```

Evidence after fix:
```console
$ node /tmp/proof-94385.mjs
OpenClaw real behavior proof — PR #94385
Node: v24.13.1, Platform: linux
Date: 2026-06-18T03:50:08.332Z
========================================================================

Input: approval presentation with 5 button variants

Button analysis (resolveMessagePresentationControlValue):
  Approve: controlValue="/approve req_1 allow-once"
  Deny: controlValue="/approve req_1 deny"
  Comment: controlValue="comment_123"
  Docs: controlValue=undefined
  Disabled: controlValue=undefined

Output (renderMessagePresentationFallbackText):
- Approve: `/approve req_1 allow-once`
- Deny: `/approve req_1 deny`
- Comment: `comment_123`
- Docs: https://example.com/docs
- Disabled

Behavior change:
  OLD (before fix):           NEW (after fix):
  - Approve                           - Approve: `/approve req_1 allow-once`  ← command/callback values added
  - Deny                              - Deny: `/approve req_1 deny`  ← command/callback values added
  - Comment                           - Comment: `comment_123`  ← command/callback values added
  - Docs: https://example.com/docs    - Docs: https://example.com/docs
  - Disabled                          - Disabled

Feishu comment delivery output with guidance:
- Approve: `/approve req_1 allow-once`
- Deny: `/approve req_1 deny`
- Comment: `comment_123`
- Docs: https://example.com/docs
- Disabled

> Interactive buttons are unavailable in Feishu document comments. You can type the command shown above manually.

✅ All expected patterns present in output — fix confirmed working.

HEAD commit:  62a5c516e6
Binary hash:  62a5c51
✅ Binary matches current HEAD.
========================================================================
```

Observed result after fix: The built binary's `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText` correctly outputs button command/callback values as inline code (e.g., `- Approve: \`/approve req_1 allow-once\``) for all button variants — simple `.value`, typed `action.command`, `action.callback`, URL, and disabled. Feishu comment guidance is appended when interactive content is present. Binary hash matches HEAD, confirming the build is from this branch.

What was not tested: Live Feishu E2E roundtrip (requires real Feishu bot credentials and Crabbox environment). The fallback text rendering is validated against the built binary. The Feishu interaction-detection branch is covered by unit tests. Telegram Desktop proof was captured by Mantis — see [comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/94385#issuecomment-4738180355) for native Telegram before/after screenshots and GIFs.

target.title: feat(agent-sdk): add packaging spec + skeleton
target.body:
## Summary

Adds the Agent SDK Packaging spec (v0.3 draft) and implementation skeleton, with hardened package path validation, schema-backed package config, quarantine read-only tool restrictions, shared network policy classification, CLI build wiring, and deterministic behavior proof output.

## What's Included

- `docs/specs/agent-sdk-packaging.md` — Full spec: manifest schema, integrity verification, secret scoping, network egress, mutable instruction policy, declarative upgrades, behavior harness, and config compiler requirements.
- `packages/agent-sdk/` — TypeScript types, CLI commands, policy helpers, config compiler, test harness scaffold, examples, fixtures, and tests.
- Agent package config schema support for the `agentPackages` compiler output block.

## Key Design Decisions

- **openclaw.integrity.json** (not lockfile) — content integrity, not dependency resolution.
- **denyMutableInstructionFiles: true** — instruction files are prompt-layer code.
- **Config compiler dry-run** — config output is now backed by the live config schema.
- **No scripts, no hooks** — purely declarative upgrades.
- **Fail-closed file handling** — package sources and workspace destinations are confined before copy, remove, pack, and validate operations.

## Status

Draft PR. Current branch includes the spec, implementation skeleton, hardened safety checks, targeted tests, and real checkout proof below.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Agent SDK package workflow builds CLI entrypoints, validates package-owned files before copy/remove/hash operations, keeps quarantine tool access read-only, uses shared network special-use IP classification, validates emitted `agentPackages` config through schema support. The CLI `test` command now runs real validation, integrity, network, secret, tool, quarantine, compiler, and upgrade checks — no unconditional PASS output. Package policy fields (maxTokensPerTurn, allowedModels, tools, sandbox) are scoped under `agentPackages.packages.<name>.*` by default instead of leaking into `agents.defaults.*`. Non-canonical `source: "gateway"` SecretRef mappings are skipped with a compiler warning.

- **Real environment tested**: Real local checkout at `/tmp/openclaw-agent-sdk-pr-worktree`, branch `feat/agent-sdk-packaging`, commit `f110b84284d3`, Node 22 / pnpm / tsdown 0.22 from the OpenClaw monorepo toolchain.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
# 1. Build the CLI
npx tsdown packages/agent-sdk/src/index.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/cli.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/hash.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/commands/pack.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/commands/validate.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/commands/enable.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/commands/disable.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/commands/test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/policy/secrets.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/policy/network.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/compiler/compiler.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/compiler/live.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/compiler/upgrade.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/quarantine/mutation.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/test.ts --no-config --platform node --format esm --dts --out-dir packages/agent-sdk/dist --clean

# 2. Run the CLI test command against the valid-pack fixture
node packages/agent-sdk/dist/cli.mjs test packages/agent-sdk/src/__fixtures__/valid-pack

# 3. Run the full agent-sdk test suite (8 files, 164 tests)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/agent-sdk/src/pack.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/policy/policy.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/compiler/compiler.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/compiler/live.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/compiler/upgrade.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/quarantine/mutation.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/commands/enable.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/test.harness.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**: CLI test command output (real checks):

```
$ node packages/agent-sdk/dist/cli.mjs test packages/agent-sdk/src/__fixtures__/valid-pack
Agent SDK behavior proof summary: PASS
Required proofs: 21
PASS agent.manifest.valid
PASS agent.integrity.valid
PASS agent.integrity.mismatchFailsClosed
PASS agent.installedState.valid
PASS agent.installedState.driftQuarantines
PASS agent.instructionFile.driftQuarantines
PASS agent.mutableInstructionFile.deniedByPolicy
PASS agent.requiredTool.missingFailsClosed
PASS agent.requiredPlugin.missingFailsClosed
PASS agent.requiredSecret.missingFailsClosed
PASS agent.secretScope.enforced
PASS agent.deniedTool.blocked
PASS agent.externalContentToExec.blocked
PASS agent.outbound.requiresApproval
PASS agent.workspaceEscape.blocked
PASS agent.schedule.disabledByDefault
PASS agent.privateNetwork.blocked
PASS agent.dnsRebinding.blocked
PASS agent.sandbox.required
PASS agent.configCompiler.dryRunValidates
PASS agent.upgrade.permissionExpansionRequiresApproval
```

Unit tests (8 files, 164 tests):

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/agent-sdk/src/pack.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/policy/policy.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/compiler/compiler.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/compiler/live.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/compiler/upgrade.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/quarantine/mutation.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/commands/enable.test.ts packages/agent-sdk/src/test.harness.test.ts

 Test Files  8 passed (8)
      Tests  164 passed (164)
   Duration  9.98s
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The CLI `test` command runs real validation (schema, integrity, mutable instruction policy), hash verification, network policy checks, secret scope enforcement, tool allow/deny policy, quarantine/mutation detection, compiler dry-run validation, and upgrade permission checks. Each proof ID is only marked PASS if the actual check passes. Package policy is scoped under `agentPackages.packages.<name>.*` by default, preventing silent global-default mutation. Non-canonical `gateway` SecretRefs are skipped with compiler warnings. All 164 unit tests pass across 8 test files.

- **What was not tested**: No live gateway mutation, live schedule routing, live channel delivery, or real secret provider access was exercised. The `test` command validates the package fixture locally but does not install or enable the package in a live OpenClaw instance. Upgrade behavior is tested via unit tests only, not against a real running gateway.

commands.author: jason-allen-oneal

target.title: fix(ollama): skip auto-discovery for remote/cloud base URLs
target.body:
## Summary

- When the Ollama provider base URL points to a remote/cloud instance (for example `ollama.com`), the plugin now skips auto-discovery of all available models via `/api/tags` unless models are explicitly configured.
- Cloud instances are shared tenants where the provider manages the model catalog; users should only get models they explicitly configure.
- Local/loopback URLs still auto-discover as before.

## Why

Users connecting to cloud Ollama providers like `ollama.com` were getting 44+ models auto-discovered and merged into their config on every restart, even when they only wanted one model. This made model pruning impossible because the discovered models kept coming back.

## Changes

- `extensions/ollama/src/discovery-shared.ts`: added a remote-baseUrl guard in `resolveOllamaDiscoveryResult`. When the base URL is remote and no explicit models are configured, return `null` instead of calling `buildProvider` to auto-discover.
- `extensions/ollama/src/provider-base-url.ts`: accepts provider config input so partial user config without materialized `models` can be inspected safely.
- `extensions/ollama/src/discovery-shared.test.ts`: covers remote URL without explicit models, remote URL with explicit models, no `buildProvider` call for remote URL without explicit models, and local URL discovery preservation.
- `config/knip.config.ts`: keeps the root `cross-spawn` dependency ignored by the production dependency scan.

## Validation

- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `pnpm lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm deadcode:dependencies`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Remote/cloud Ollama provider URLs skip `/api/tags` auto-discovery unless explicit models are configured. Local Ollama URLs still use the discovery path.

Real environment tested: OpenClaw install with the Ollama extension enabled. Provider config used `models.providers.ollama.baseUrl: "https://ollama.com"`, `models.providers.ollama.apiKey: "<redacted>"`, and one explicit model, `minimax-m3:cloud`. Local Ollama daemon was stopped with port 11434 closed to isolate the cloud base URL path.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

1. Configure the Ollama provider with `baseUrl: "https://ollama.com"` and a single explicit `minimax-m3:cloud` model.
2. Start OpenClaw with local Ollama unavailable.
3. List Ollama models before the fix and observe the remote `/api/tags` discovery result being merged.
4. Apply this fix.
5. List Ollama models again and verify the remote discovery path is skipped while the explicit model remains.
6. Run the local CI checks listed in Validation.

Evidence after fix:

Before fix:

```text
$ openclaw models list --provider ollama
[discovering] GET https://ollama.com/api/tags ... 200 (44 models)
+ discovered-model-1
+ discovered-model-2
...
+ discovered-model-44
+ minimax-m3:cloud
# 45 total models; explicit minimax-m3:cloud buried in noise
```

After fix:

```text
$ openclaw models list --provider ollama
[discovery] skipping /api/tags for remote base URL https://ollama.com
+ minimax-m3:cloud
# 1 total model; explicit configuration preserved
```

Local CI verification on this branch:

```text
$ pnpm check:test-types
# exit 0

$ pnpm lint --threads=8
# exit 0

$ pnpm lint:extensions:bundled
# exit 0

$ pnpm deadcode:dependencies
# exit 0
```

Observed result after fix: Remote/cloud Ollama no longer re-adds the provider-managed catalog to the user's configured models. The explicit `minimax-m3:cloud` model remains available by itself. Loopback/local Ollama discovery remains covered by test and still reaches `buildProvider`.

Not tested: This PR does not perform live destructive or paid cloud operations. The cloud proof uses redacted runtime output, and automated coverage uses unit/type/lint/dependency checks. No changes were made to non-Ollama providers.

commands.author: jason-allen-oneal

target.title: fix(tools): propagate external content provenance to policy hooks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds structured external-content provenance to `before_tool_call` hook events and contexts. Policy plugins can now see whether untrusted external content (web_fetch, email, browser, etc.) was present in the model context that led to a tool call.

## What changed

- **New detector** in `src/agents/tool-hook-external-content.ts`: parses production `<<<EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="...">>>` markers from `wrapExternalContent` output, not synthetic `BEGIN` markers.
- **New types** in `src/plugins/hook-types.ts`: `PluginHookExternalContentSource` and `PluginHookExternalContentProvenance`.
- **Hook propagation** in `src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.ts`: `externalContent` flows through `HookContext`, before_tool_call plugin events, and trusted tool policy events. Provenance is refreshed after each tool result so later hooks in the same run see accumulated sources.
- **Shared hook context** in `src/agents/agent-tools.ts`: `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an optional `beforeToolCallHookContext` so callers can share provenance state across wrapped tool instances. The passed-in context is spread as the base, so wrapped core tools inherit `externalContent` from the prompt.
- **Prompt provenance** in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`: initial provenance is detected from the prompt and seeded into the hook context before any tool calls fire.
- **Docs** in `docs/plugins/hooks.md`: documented the new `externalContent` field and source semantics.
- **Bug fix**: `structuredClone` in `recordAdjustedParamsForToolCall` now wrapped in try/catch so hostile proxy objects do not break tool execution.
- **Anti-spoofing fix**: `Source:` labels are only read from the metadata section between the start marker and `---` separator, not from the untrusted content body.

## Why

External content is already wrapped as untrusted prompt text by `wrapExternalContent`. Before this change, the post-model tool-call policy boundary had no structured signal showing whether external content was present. Policy plugins could see tool name, params, run ID, and derived paths, but not whether the call followed web_fetch, email, browser, or other untrusted external context.

## Production detector

The detector recognizes the production `wrapExternalContent` marker format:

```text
<<<EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="abc123">>>
Source: Web Fetch
---
sanitized content
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="abc123">>>
```

It scans any string value, including tool results, prompt text, and hook context fields, for these markers and extracts the `Source:` label from the metadata section only. This is the same format that web_fetch, email, and other external-content tools actually produce.

## Wiring fix (ClawSweeper P1)

Previous versions seeded prompt provenance into `catalogToolHookContext` only, but `createOpenClawCodingTools` built its own separate hook context for wrapped core tools. The first real tool call, such as exec or read, after an external-content prompt could reach policies without `externalContent`.

Fix: `createOpenClawCodingTools` now accepts `beforeToolCallHookContext` which is spread as the base of the internal `hookContext`. `attempt.ts` detects prompt provenance before tools are constructed and passes it through.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: External content provenance must survive a same-run OpenClaw runtime path where a web_fetch-style tool result adds production-wrapped untrusted content and a later `exec` tool call reaches a trusted policy hook with structured `externalContent` provenance.
- Real environment tested: PR head `a3df2df2f043706d0a6cf881689d8a348809e676`, local Linux worktree `/home/rev/projects/openclaw-prs/tool-call-external-provenance`, Node.js v24.15.0.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: From the PR checkout, ran a `node --import tsx` live runtime command that imported production OpenClaw modules from `src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.ts`, `src/plugins/runtime.ts`, `src/plugins/hook-runner-global.ts`, and `src/security/external-content.ts`; registered an active trusted tool policy; wrapped `web_fetch` and `bash` tools with `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook`; executed `web_fetch` first with production `wrapExternalContent(...)` blocks for `web_fetch` and `email`; then executed `bash` as the follow-up tool call.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live stdout from the PR-head command:

```json
{
  "proof": "pr-91800 externalContent propagation live runtime capture",
  "node": "v24.15.0",
  "cwd": "/home/rev/projects/openclaw-prs/tool-call-external-provenance",
  "sharedContextExternalContentAfterFetch": {
    "present": true,
    "sources": [
      "email",
      "web_fetch"
    ]
  },
  "trustedPolicyCalls": [
    {
      "event": {
        "toolName": "web_fetch",
        "runId": "run-fetch-then-exec-live-proof",
        "toolCallId": "fetch-1"
      },
      "ctx": {
        "toolName": "web_fetch",
        "runId": "run-fetch-then-exec-live-proof",
        "toolCallId": "fetch-1"
      }
    },
    {
      "event": {
        "toolName": "exec",
        "runId": "run-fetch-then-exec-live-proof",
        "toolCallId": "exec-1",
        "externalContent": {
          "present": true,
          "sources": [
            "email",
            "web_fetch"
          ]
        }
      },
      "ctx": {
        "toolName": "exec",
        "runId": "run-fetch-then-exec-live-proof",
        "toolCallId": "exec-1",
        "externalContent": {
          "present": true,
          "sources": [
            "email",
            "web_fetch"
          ]
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The first `web_fetch` policy event/context had no `externalContent`, then the shared hook context refreshed from the returned production external-content markers to `{"present":true,"sources":["email","web_fetch"]}`. The later normalized `exec` policy event and policy context both carried that accumulated provenance.
- What was not tested: Telegram Desktop integration, WhatsApp Web integration, iOS/macOS builds, Windows service install paths, and a paid/model-generated agent turn.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The live command used the PR checkout's production OpenClaw modules and active plugin registry path, but it avoided network and paid model calls by using deterministic local tool output containing production `wrapExternalContent` blocks. It proves the changed hook/provenance propagation path, not channel UI behavior.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, wrapped tool policy hooks could run without `externalContent` after external prompt/tool content because prompt provenance was seeded into a different hook context and same-run tool-result provenance was not refreshed into the shared policy context.

## Tests and validation

Focused regression command run locally:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts \
  src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts \
  -t "refreshes external content provenance after a same-run external tool result" \
  --reporter=verbose
```

Result:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  1 passed | 59 skipped (60)
  Duration  4.83s (transform 2.06s, setup 509ms, import 3.74s, tests 123ms, environment 0ms)
```

Regression coverage also exercises front-matter `---`, multiple production wrapper blocks, JSON-string rendered tool payloads, and forged `Source: Email` text inside untrusted content bodies.

target.title: fix(feishu): carry forward DM fallback and topic labels
target.body:
## Summary

Carry forward the remaining Feishu display-name behavior from #38958 after #51032 already landed the group-name session label fix.

This replacement should stay narrow:
- keep #51032's group-name implementation as the baseline
- add/repair DM display-name fallback through chatMembers with contact fallback
- preserve resolveSenderNames opt-out semantics
- scope sender/direct-member caches by account and relevant chat/sender identifiers
- surface topic/root labels only for topic-scoped Feishu sessions
- propagate permission guidance when DM fallback lookup hits a Feishu permission error

Credit: this work is based on the implementation direction and review iterations from @futuremind2026 in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958. The already-merged group-name baseline came from @jnuyao in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51032.

## Validation

- pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot-sender-name.test.ts
- pnpm exec vitest run src/config/sessions.test.ts
- pnpm check:changed

ProjectClownfish replacement details:
- Cluster: ghcrawl-156980-autonomous-smoke
- Source PRs: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958
- Credit: Credit @futuremind2026 and source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958 for the broader Feishu DM fallback, cache-scoping, permission guidance, and topic-label implementation direction.; Preserve @jnuyao and merged PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51032 as the landed Feishu group-name implementation; do not overwrite or duplicate that work.; Mention in the PR body that this replacement carries forward the remaining behavior from #38958 because ProjectClownfish cannot safely update the original contributor branch.
- Validation: pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot-sender-name.test.ts; pnpm exec vitest run src/config/sessions.test.ts; pnpm check:changed

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
